key_insight: Phosphorus doping plus an oxygen vacancy synergistically tunes CeO2(111) electronic structure and defect chemistry, making formaldehyde adsorption and C–H activation much easier.
engineering_relevance: P-doped defective ceria could enable more efficient low-temperature formaldehyde oxidation catalysts with faster regeneration and lower operating barriers.
method: Static DFT on pristine, P-doped, and vacancy-containing CeO2(111) surfaces
Dynamic DFT simulations to assess adsorption, reaction barriers, and product desorption
Electronic-structure analysis of band gap change and Ce3+ state formation
evidence_signal: The abstract reports a band-gap reduction from 2.26 to 2.09 eV, adsorption strengthening from -0.62 to -2.65 eV, and a 0.84 eV lower C–H cleavage barrier on the defective P-doped surface.
watch_item: These conclusions are based on first-principles surface models, so real catalysts may differ due to particle size, coverage, temperature, and support effects.
paper_title: Synergistic Effects of Phosphorus Doping and Oxygen Vacancies on Formaldehyde Oxidation over CeO$_2$(111): A First Principles Investigation
